# Industry Intel — April 29, 2026

## 🚨 1. Photohawk Emerges as Direct FMS Competitor — Bib + Face AI, Sports-First

**What:** Photohawk (photohawk.com) is an AI-powered photo recognition platform built specifically for sports events, offering both bib detection and facial recognition search. Their pitch to participants: "Simply enter your bib number or take a quick selfie, and our AI instantly pulls up every photo of you from the entire event — start line, on course, and finish." They're positioning for photographers and organizers, not just consumers, and appear to be actively signing up events beyond running (pickleball, school sports).

**Why it matters to FMS:** Photohawk is building the same core stack as FMS (bib + face search, mobile-first, event commerce) without the exclusivity model. If they're signing organizers in markets FMS hasn't locked up yet — specifically SEA — they could become the "already there" incumbent before FMS expands. Their lack of a prepay/registration integration model is the key gap FMS still has as a moat.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Van or BizDev should audit Photohawk's organizer list and pricing. Add to competitor dossiers at `findmyshots/competitors/photohawk.md`. Check if they have any SEA clients or PH presence before FMS's Thailand/Malaysia push.
🔗 https://photohawk.com/

## 🚨 2. ActivePix — "Reinventing Race Photography for the Digital Age" (New Entrant Watch)

**What:** ActivePix (activepix.com) describes itself as building "technology that transforms the race day experience for participants, organisers and sponsors" — positioning as more than a photo platform. Their about page signals a three-sided model (participants, organizers, sponsors) which mirrors FMS's architecture but adds explicit sponsor monetization. Appeared in event technology search results alongside Photohawk and Zenfolio, suggesting growing visibility.

**Why it matters to FMS:** The sponsor-side monetization angle is one FMS hasn't developed yet. If ActivePix is pitching organizers on "we bring you photo revenue *and* sponsor revenue," that's a more compelling deal than FMS's current commission model — especially for organizers still undecided after Rio's deal closes.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Tim or BizDev should explore ActivePix's product depth — is sponsor integration real or just positioning? If real, Van needs to roadmap a sponsor/brand activation feature within Video Highlights or as a standalone offering.
🔗 https://activepix.com/about

## 💡 3. Real-Time AI Photo Delivery Becomes 2026 Event Standard — Arms Race Is On

**What:** Eventifai's 2026 planning guide reports that real-time AI photo delivery — where event photos are automatically uploaded, tagged, and pushed to participants' phones within minutes of being taken — is becoming a standard expectation for event photographers. AI auto-sorts, applies face recognition, and delivers direct to mobile apps or email. The guide cites "enhanced guest engagement" and "social media amplification" as the key organizer value props.

**Why it matters to FMS:** FMS currently delivers photos post-event (photos purchasable after the race). If real-time delivery becomes the norm among Western platforms, SEA organizers will start expecting it. FMS's prepay integration (Phase 2) partially addresses this — but real-time delivery is a different UX advantage that competitors could use to win organizers.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Tim should flag real-time delivery on the product roadmap — even a "photos ready within 2 hours" SLA would be a differentiator in PH/SEA. This doesn't need to be Phase 2 scope but should be on the 2026 H2 roadmap.
🔗 https://www.eventifai.com/planning-guides/real-time-photo-delivery-how-2026-event-photographers-use-ai-for-instant-sharing

---

## ⚠️ 4. Australian Open 2026 Facial Recognition Triggers Privacy Commissioner Warning

**What:** The 2026 Australian Open deployed opt-in facial recognition entry via its "AO Express Entry" platform — fans upload a selfie to their Ticketmaster account and walk through express gates. Australia's Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind publicly criticized the system, saying event organizers were "putting the onus on people to monitor their privacy when it should be the other way around." Concerns raised: implicit consent via ticketing T&Cs, data retention policies, and cloud storage security.

**Why it matters to FMS:** FMS uses AWS Rekognition for face search, which means a privacy incident (or new PH/SEA regulation) could force a consent flow rework. The Australian Open case is a preview of the regulatory pressure coming for any platform using biometric data at mass events. Philippines' Data Privacy Act (RA 10173) has biometric provisions FMS should already be complying with.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Tim should conduct a quick DPA compliance review for FMS's face recognition feature — specifically: is consent explicitly obtained per-participant, is data retention policy documented, and can users opt out? Document this before US market launch where biometric laws (IL BIPA, TX, WA) are stricter.
🔗 https://www.ausleisure.com.au/news/facial-recognition-and-biometric-technology-2026-australian-open-sparks-privacy-and-security-debate

---

## 🌏 5. Phuket Marathon June 2026 — SEA's Biggest Island Race Is Unclaimed Territory

**What:** Phuket's largest sporting event, the Phuket Marathon, runs June 13–14, 2026, and draws thousands of international runners from across Asia. It's listed among the top sports events in SEA for 2026 alongside regional mega-events. Photo coverage at the Phuket Marathon is largely handled by local freelancers with no structured photo commerce platform — the classic FB group/Google Drive problem that FMS was built to solve.

**Why it matters to FMS:** Thailand is FMS's first SEA target market (per GOALS.md). Phuket Marathon June 2026 is the perfect pilot event — high international attendance, underdeveloped photo commerce, and close enough to get a local photographer partner without a full market build-out. If FMS covers this event, it becomes the SEA reference customer story for the pitch deck.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Lenli should reach out to Phuket Marathon organizers this week with a "no cost to organizer, professional photo experience" pitch. Even a non-exclusive pilot sets the SEA beachhead 6 weeks from now. Identify 1 Thai photographer partner to brief before approaching.
🔗 https://www.augustman.com/my/entertainment/culture/events/sports-events-happening-across-southeast-asia-in-2026/

## 💰 6. Philippines QR Payments Hit 60% of Digital Transactions — FMS Checkout Tailwind

**What:** GCash and other Philippine digital wallet providers reported in February 2026 that QR-based payments now account for approximately 60% of all digital transactions in the Philippines. The country's digital wallet market is described as "one of the fastest-growing segments" of PH fintech, with cashless payments increasingly integrated into everyday financial activities for millions of Filipinos.

**Why it matters to FMS:** FMS's photo checkout is a digital transaction. As GCash/QR becomes the default payment method for millions of PH runners, friction at checkout drops — meaning the gap between "I found my photo" and "I paid for my photo" gets smaller. This directly supports the conversion rate improvement FMS is targeting from 1.24% (March) → 20% (Phase 1) → 80% (Phase 2).

**👉 Action/Watch:** Confirm GCash QR checkout is live and prominent in FMS's payment flow. If it's buried or not the default for PH users, Tim should surface it. This is free conversion rate lift.
🔗 https://vocal.media/trader/philippines-digital-wallet-market-2026-cashless-payments-fintech-innovation-and-financial-inclusion

## 🧠 7. SaaS Founder's Viral B2B Event ROI Framework — Direct Playbook for Chicago Meetings

**What:** A LinkedIn post by Ashley Dukat (CMP) outlining six rules for SaaS founders attending B2B events went viral in the community. Key rules: (1) Treat it as a 12-week campaign (6 weeks before, 6 weeks after), (2) Set hard ROI metrics — meetings booked, pipeline created, (3) **Pre-book meetings before you arrive**, (4) Add value on-stand with demos/mini-workshops, (5) Align sales and marketing so hot leads don't go cold, (6) Run a hurdle model — ask if the same budget spent on ABM or client dinners would deliver more.

**Why it matters to FMS:** Van's team is going to Chicago for enterprise sales meetings next week. This framework is the exact playbook they should be running — especially Rule 3 (pre-book meetings) and Rule 5 (hot leads can't go cold). Without a structured follow-up plan baked in before the trip, the Chicago meetings risk being high-cost, low-conversion.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Van should confirm: (1) Are Chicago meetings pre-booked or being set up on arrival? (2) Is there a 6-week post-visit follow-up plan? (3) Who owns follow-up — Van, Lenli, or BizDev? Treat Chicago as a campaign, not a trip.
🔗 https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ashley-dukat_b2b-events-everything-saas-founders-need-activity-7374494964459913216-tRqU

## 🌏 8. ASEAN Cross-Border QR Payments Now Live Between Multiple Countries — SEA Commerce Tailwind

**What:** An IMF Staff Country Report (April 2026) on ASEAN's digital payment revolution confirms that bilateral cross-border QR payment linkages are now live between multiple ASEAN countries — enabling real-time local currency payments across borders without USD conversion. Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and the Philippines are part of this network. The report notes that local-currency QR payments are growing faster than card-based cross-border payments.

**Why it matters to FMS:** When FMS expands to Thailand and Malaysia, participants from other SEA countries attending those events (e.g., a PH runner doing Phuket Marathon) can pay in their local currency via QR with no conversion friction. This removes a key barrier to multi-country photo commerce that previously required Stripe or international card support.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Tim should investigate whether FMS's payment stack supports cross-border QR (e.g., GCash x DuitNow x PromptPay integrations). This is a Q3 2026 enabler for SEA expansion — don't build it from scratch if a payment aggregator already handles it.
🔗 https://www.elibrary.imf.org/view/journals/002/2026/042/article-A002-en.xml

## 📅 9. Triathlon + OCR Participation Surging in 2026 — Women Driving New Growth Wave

**What:** Multiple reports confirm 2026 is a breakout year for triathlon and obstacle course racing (OCR). Ironman, T100, Challenge Family, and XTERRA all reported significant participation increases over 2024. Women's participation is highlighted as the defining trend — triathlon experts predict 2026 "will be the year the sport is reshaped by" female athletes. OCR events (Spartan, Tough Mudder variants) are described as "some of the fastest-growing trends in the endurance world."

**Why it matters to FMS:** FMS already has Spartan SEA through Rio's deal (pending signing). The women's participation surge is relevant because female runners historically over-index on photo purchasing behavior — they're more likely to share race photos on social, making conversion rates higher on female-dominated events. FMS should be targeting women-led events and running clubs as a priority sales segment.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Lenli should add women-focused running events and clubs (e.g., She Runs PH, women's-only races) to the organizer outreach pipeline. Frame the FMS pitch around social sharing data — if FMS has internal data on female buyer conversion rates, surface it for the sales deck.
🔗 https://www.triathlete.com/culture/news/5-predictions-for-the-triathlon-world-in-2026/

## 💰 10. Philippine Startup Funding at 6-Year Low — Investor Landscape Tighter for SEA Seed Rounds

**What:** Philippine startup funding fell to a six-year low in 2025, according to StartupMachine's analysis, though the ecosystem notes that "$6.3B in value was created" — suggesting valuations held even as check sizes declined. The overall SEA funding environment is selective, with investors favoring companies with clear unit economics and B2B revenue models over consumer growth plays.

**Why it matters to FMS:** If FMS raises external capital post-spinout, it's entering a tighter funding environment. The good news: FMS's model (B2B organizer partnerships, clear take rate economics, prepay integration path) is exactly what SEA investors want to see in 2026 — not another consumer app burning cash for growth. The Rio deal and Phase 2 projections are FMS's fundraising narrative.

**👉 Action/Watch:** Van should ensure the FMS pitch deck is investor-ready before Q3 — with Rio Phase 1 actuals (post-signing), Phase 2 projections, and the prepay integration timeline clearly laid out. Dave should review the narrative. Even if FMS isn't raising now, being raise-ready means better terms when the moment comes.
🔗 https://www.linkedin.com/posts/startupmachine_startupmachine-daily-2-philippine-startups-activity-7442114228209000450-DVkc
